title: Real Estate Finance and Investment Artificial Intelligence (ReFiAI)
description: A comprehensive course on Real Estate Finance, from basics to advanced topics.

parts.title: Real Estate 101
parts.description: Foundational skills for Real Estate professionals.

units.title: Real Estate Economics
units.description: How the real estate system works and what sets prices: the space, asset and development markets, and the cap rate.

sections.content:
## What you'll be able to do

- Describe the three interacting markets that make up the real estate system.
- Explain the feedback loop that links rents, prices and new construction.
- Define the **cap rate** and use it to price a property.
- List what makes cap rates rise or fall.

> This is the free Real Estate 101 track — the foundation for everything in the paid course.

## Three markets, one system

Real estate is not just buildings — it is a system of three connected markets:

1. **Space market** — landlords and tenants trade the right to *use* space. This sets **rents**.
2. **Asset market** — investors trade *ownership* of buildings. This sets **prices** and **cap rates**.
3. **Development industry** — converts capital into new buildings when prices justify construction cost.

### The feedback loop
High demand pushes **rents** up → higher rents lift **asset values** → when values rise above **construction cost**, developers **build** → new supply eventually moderates rents. Round and round.
Knowledge Check — The System

quizData.text: Which market sets the cap rate?
quizData.explanation: The asset market is where ownership trades, so prices — and the yields/cap rates investors demand — are set there.

quizData.text: What happens when property values rise above construction cost?
quizData.explanation: When value > cost it is profitable to build, so the development industry adds new supply.

lessons.title: Cap Rates: The Price of Real Estate

sections.content:
## The cap rate

The **capitalization rate** is the fundamental price metric of real estate. It is the ratio of a property's net operating income to its value:

`Cap Rate = NOI / Value`  →  `Value = NOI / Cap Rate`

A building earning €60,000 of NOI at a 6% cap rate is worth €60,000 / 0.06 = **€1,000,000**. A *lower* cap rate means a *higher* price per euro of income.

## What determines cap rates?

Three forces:

1. **Opportunity cost of capital** — the return available on similar-risk investments (anchored by the risk-free rate, e.g. government bonds).
2. **Growth expectations** for the property's future cash flows — higher expected growth → lower cap rate → higher price.
3. **Risk** — riskier income (short leases, weak market) → higher cap rate → lower price.

A useful shorthand: `Cap Rate = Risk-free rate + Risk premium − Growth`.

### Try it

Adjust NOI and the cap rate below and watch the value move.
Knowledge Check — Cap Rates

quizData.text: Cap rates in a market are 10.5%. A property earns €50,000 net income per year. What should it sell for?
Other things equal, which property would have the LOWEST cap rate?
options: Short leases in a declining market
Long leases in a declining market
Long leases in a growing market
quizData.correctAnswer: Long leases in a growing market
quizData.explanation: Lower risk (long, secure leases) plus higher growth both push the cap rate down — and the price up.

quizData.text: If investors' required return falls, all else equal, prices:
quizData.explanation: Price = NOI / cap rate, and the cap rate tracks the required return. A lower required return lowers the cap rate and raises prices.

sections.content:
## The 4-quadrant model (DiPasquale–Wheaton)

The classic model links the four pieces of the system on one diagram: rent determination (space market), valuation via the cap rate (asset market), construction (development), and stock adjustment (back to space).

A jump in **demand** raises rents → raises prices → triggers construction → adds stock → partly reverses the rent rise, settling at a new long-run equilibrium with more space and higher rents than before.

## Do interest rates drive cap rates?

Theory says higher interest rates should raise cap rates (higher discount rate → lower prices). In practice the link is **weak and unstable**: over long periods cap rates and the 10-year government rate have only a loose, sometimes *negative*, relationship. Capital flows, risk appetite and growth expectations often dominate. The lesson: interest rates matter, but they are one input among several, not a switch.
Knowledge Check — The Cycle

quizData.text: In the 4-quadrant model, a rise in space demand first causes:
quizData.explanation: Demand lifts rents and prices; only then does construction respond, eventually adding stock.

quizData.text: The empirical relationship between cap rates and interest rates is:
quizData.explanation: History shows a loose link; growth expectations and capital flows often outweigh rates.

sections.content:
## One-page summary

- Real estate = **space market** (rents) + **asset market** (prices/cap rates) + **development** (new supply), joined by a feedback loop.
- **Value = NOI / Cap Rate.** Lower cap rate = higher price.
- Cap rates fall with **lower required returns** and **higher growth**, and rise with **more risk**.
- Interest rates influence but do not mechanically set cap rates.
Unit Assessment — Real Estate Economics

quizData.text: A property's NOI is €80,000 and the market cap rate is 5%. Its value is:
Which is NOT one of the three markets in the real estate system?
quizData.explanation: The three are the space, asset and development markets.

resources.title: Real Estate System & Cap Rate — slides

units.title: Present Value Math for Real Estate
units.description: The time value of money and the Excel toolkit (PV, NPV, IRR, PMT) every real estate model is built on.

sections.content:
## Why a euro today beats a euro tomorrow

Money available now is worth more than the same amount later, because it can be invested to earn a return. This single idea underpins every valuation.

### Present value
`PV = FV / (1 + r)^n`

where **FV** is the future amount, **r** the discount rate, and **n** the number of periods. A higher discount rate (more risk, or higher opportunity cost) gives a **lower** present value.

### Quick example (euros)

You will receive **€10,000** in 3 years. At a 7% discount rate:

`PV = 10,000 / 1.07³ = 10,000 / 1.225 = €8,163`

The further away the cash flow, or the higher the rate, the smaller today's value.
Knowledge Check — TVM

quizData.text: If the discount rate rises, the present value of a future cash flow:
quizData.explanation: A larger denominator (1+r)^n shrinks the present value.

quizData.text: Present value mainly captures the idea that:
options: Money earns a return over time
quizData.correctAnswer: Money earns a return over time
quizData.explanation: TVM reflects the earning capacity (opportunity cost) of money.

lessons.title: PV, NPV, IRR & PMT in Excel

sections.content:
## The four functions you will use constantly

- `=PV(rate, nper, pmt, [fv])` — present value of a stream.
- `=NPV(rate, values)` — present value of a series of cash flows (assumes the first is one period away).
- `=IRR(values)` — the discount rate that makes NPV = 0 (your return).
- `=PMT(rate, nper, pv)` — the level payment that amortises a loan.

> Convention: assume cash flows occur at the **end** of each period unless told otherwise. And remember Excel's `NPV` does **not** include time-0 — add the initial outlay separately.

### Hands-on lab

Use the spreadsheet to practise discounting cash flows to a present value.
Knowledge Check — Excel

quizData.text: Which function returns the level payment on a loan?
quizData.explanation: PMT computes the constant payment for a loan given rate, term and principal.

quizData.text: IRR is the rate at which:
options: The cap rate equals the discount rate
quizData.explanation: IRR is the discount rate that sets the net present value of the cash flows to zero.

sections.content:
## One-page summary

- **PV = FV / (1+r)ⁿ.** Higher rate or longer wait → lower value.
- **NPV** sums discounted cash flows; **IRR** is the rate where NPV = 0.
- **PMT** gives the amortising loan payment.
- Cash flows are end-of-period by default; add the time-0 outlay outside Excel's `NPV`.
Unit Assessment — Present Value Math

quizData.text: The PV of €1,000 received in 2 years at 5% is closest to:
To value a finite stream of differing annual cash flows you would use:
quizData.explanation: NPV discounts a series of (possibly varying) cash flows to today.

units.title: Finance for Real Estate
units.description: Risk and return, the building blocks of asset pricing, and how the cap rate decomposes into a required return and growth.

sections.content:
## Required return = compensation for risk

Investors price assets on the relationship between **expected risk** and **required return**. Start from the **risk-free rate** (government bonds) and add a **risk premium** for the extra risks of real estate — illiquidity, leverage, and asset-specific risk.

`Required Return (r) = Risk-free rate + Risk premium`

Return itself has two parts: **income return** (the yield from NOI, contractual and stable) and **appreciation return** (price growth, market-driven and riskier).
Knowledge Check — Risk & Return

quizData.text: Which component of return is generally less risky?
quizData.explanation: Income comes from contractual leases; appreciation depends on uncertain future market conditions.

quizData.text: The required return is built from:
quizData.explanation: You add a premium for real estate's risks to the risk-free baseline.

sections.content:
## R₀ = r − g

When a property behaves like a growing perpetuity, the cap rate is the required return **minus** the expected growth in cash flows:

`Cap Rate (R₀) = Required return (r) − Growth (g)`

So two properties with the same NOI can trade at very different prices: the one with higher expected growth (or lower risk) has a **lower** cap rate and a **higher** price. This is why prime, growing markets look 'expensive' on a cap-rate basis — the market is pricing in growth.
Knowledge Check — Decomposition

quizData.text: Required return is 9% and expected growth is 3%. The cap rate is:
Higher expected growth, all else equal, makes the cap rate:
quizData.explanation: More growth lowers the cap rate (R₀ = r − g) and raises the price.

sections.content:
## Don't put it all in one building

A single property carries **idiosyncratic risk** — a bad tenant, a local downturn. Spreading equity across more assets (often funded by **leverage**) reduces that risk because their cash flows are not perfectly correlated. Investors are rewarded mainly for **systematic** (market-wide) risk they cannot diversify away — the core idea behind the **CAPM** view of required returns.

*Practical takeaway:* eight buildings at 75% LTV diversify far better than two bought all-cash with the same equity — provided the leverage is prudent.
Knowledge Check — Diversification

quizData.text: Diversification reduces risk because asset cash flows are:
quizData.explanation: Imperfect correlation means losses in one asset are partly offset by others.

quizData.text: Investors are primarily compensated for:
quizData.explanation: Idiosyncratic risk can be diversified away, so the market rewards bearing systematic risk.

parts.title: REFI (Core)
parts.description: The main Real Estate Finance & Investment curriculum.

units.title: Basic Real Estate Investment Model
units.description: How income-producing property is valued and what drives investment returns — from the two components of return to a full levered DCF.

sections.content:
## What you'll be able to do

By the end of this unit you will be able to:

- Decompose total return into **income return** (yield) and **appreciation return** (growth).
- Value a property two ways — **direct capitalization** and **discounted cash flow (DCF)**.
- Read the relationship **R₀ = r − g** and explain what moves a cap rate.
- Build a clean **PGI → NOI** proforma and project it forward.
- Layer in **debt** and judge whether leverage is positive or negative for a deal.

> Estimated time for the unit: ~90 minutes. Prerequisite: *Unit 0.2 — Present Value Math*.

## Defining “return”

*Return* is the change in price of an investment over time, plus any income it generates. It can be positive (the investment was profitable) or negative (a loss). It is usually expressed as a percentage: profit divided by the amount originally invested.

Two ideas matter from the start:

1. **Price and return are opposite sides of the same coin.** As the price you pay rises, your return falls — and vice versa. An investor who needs a higher return has to pay a lower price.
2. **Money is made when you buy, not when you sell.** Once your purchase price is set, so is your expected return. But that expected return is not fully in your control: future cash flows depend on the market, so be prepared for volatility.

## The two components of return

When a property produces rent **and** changes in value, total return over a period combines two pieces:

`Rₜ = CFₜ / Vₜ₋₁  +  (Vₜ − Vₜ₋₁) / Vₜ₋₁`

- **Income return (yield)** — the return from net operating income: `yₜ = CFₜ / Vₜ₋₁`. It comes from contractual leases, so it is relatively stable.
- **Appreciation return (growth)** — the return from the rise in market value: `gₜ = (Vₜ − Vₜ₋₁) / Vₜ₋₁`. It depends on future market conditions, so it carries most of the risk.

**Total return = income return + appreciation return.**

### Worked example (from class)

A property is bought for **€200,000** at t = 0. The landlord receives **€7,000** rent and sells for **€205,000** at t = 1.

- Income return = 7,000 / 200,000 = **3.5%**
- Appreciation return = (205,000 − 200,000) / 200,000 = **2.5%**
- **Total return = 3.5% + 2.5% = 6.0%**

For multi-period investments (the norm in real estate, which is long-term), we measure return with the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — the single rate that discounts all the investment's future cash flows back to a present value equal to its purchase price.
Knowledge Check — Components of Return

quizData.text: A property yields 5% in income and appreciates by 3% over the year. What is the total return?
quizData.explanation: Total return = income return (5%) + appreciation return (3%) = 8%.

quizData.text: Which component of return is generally considered less risky?
quizData.explanation: Income comes from contractual leases; appreciation depends on future market conditions and the exit cap rate, so it carries more risk.

sections.content:
## Two roads from income to value

There are two standard approaches to valuing income property.

### 1 · Income capitalization (cap rate)

Used when cash flows are treated as an **infinite / perpetual** stream. It cannot be used for a finite horizon.

`Value = NOI₁ / Cap Rate`

where NOI₁ is the net operating income one year after purchase. The cap rate captures the **opportunity cost of capital** of a similar investment — it is read off comparable property transactions in the market.

### 2 · Discounted cash flow (DCF)

Used for a **finite** hold (e.g. 5–10 years). Value is the present value of all future cash flows — rents plus the net sale price — discounted at the investor's required total return *r*.

`Value = Σ CFₜ / (1+r)ⁿ  +  NSP / (1+r)ⁿ`

## Splitting the cap rate: R₀ = r − g

To the extent a property behaves like a **constant-growth annuity**, the cap rate is simply the required return minus the expected growth in cash flows:

`R₀ = r − g`

where `r` (= Y₀) is the discount rate / required total return and `g` is the growth rate of NOI.

> This is why a **falling required return raises prices**: if *r* drops, the cap rate R₀ falls, and since Value = NOI₁ / R₀, value goes up. The same happens if growth expectations *g* rise.

## Building the proforma (PGI → NOI)

Every model starts from the income waterfall. Work down from gross potential rent to the cash flow available to investors:

- **Potential Gross Income (PGI)** — contract + market rent
- **− Vacancy & collection loss** (a % of PGI)
- **= Effective Gross Income (EGI)**
- **− Operating Expenses** (a % of EGI)
- **= Net Operating Income (NOI)**
- **− Capital reserve (CapEx)**
- **= Property Before-Tax Cash Flow (PBTCF)**

Order matters: vacancy comes off *gross* rent; operating expenses come off *effective* income. Mixing these up is the most common proforma mistake.
Knowledge Check — Valuation

quizData.text: Cap rates in a market are 10.5%. A property earns €50,000 of net income per year. What should it sell for?
quizData.explanation: Value = NOI ÷ cap rate = 50,000 ÷ 0.105 = €476,190. Direct capitalization treats stabilized income as a perpetuity.

quizData.text: All else equal, the required rate of return on real estate falls. What happens to prices?
quizData.explanation: Price = NOI ÷ cap rate, and cap rate = r − g. A lower r lowers the cap rate, which raises value.

quizData.text: Which valuation approach is appropriate for a finite 7-year hold?
quizData.explanation: Direct capitalization assumes a perpetual stream; a finite hold is valued with DCF — PV of the interim cash flows plus the net sale price.

sections.content:
## Interactive lab

This is the in-browser version of the class Excel model. Drag any assumption and the proforma, both valuations, and the levered return recompute instantly.

Try these experiments:

1. Lower the **going-in cap rate** from 6.5% to 5.5% — watch value jump (you're paying more per € of income).
2. Set the **exit cap rate** above the going-in rate — see the DCF value fall below the direct-cap value.
3. Push the **mortgage rate** above the cap rate — the insight flips from *positive* to *negative* leverage.

### Reading the leverage result

Leverage is **positive** when the cost of debt is **below** the property's cap rate — each borrowed euro earns more than it costs, lifting equity IRR. It turns **negative** when the mortgage rate rises above the cap rate, and debt starts diluting returns. Lenders also watch the **DSCR** (NOI ÷ debt service); most want at least 1.25×.

## Hands-on: the class Excel model

Now rebuild the model yourself. The lab below is a lightweight present-value sandbox; the full class workbook (empty-cells and solutions) is in the downloads at the bottom.

Key Excel functions: `=PV(rate, nper, pmt, [fv])`, `=NPV(rate, values)`, `=IRR(values)`, and `=PMT(rate, nper, pv)` for the mortgage. Assume cash flows occur at the **end** of each period unless told otherwise.

resources.title: Basic Property Investment Model — class Excel

sections.content:
## One-page summary

- **Total return = income + appreciation.** Income is contractual and stable; appreciation carries the risk.
- **Direct cap:** Value = NOI₁ / R₀, and R₀ = r − g. Falling *r* (or rising *g*) raises value.
- **DCF** discounts each year's cash flow plus the net sale price at *r* — used for finite holds.
- **Proforma order:** vacancy off PGI, opex off EGI, to reach NOI.
- **Leverage** is positive when the cost of debt is below the cap rate — it lifts equity IRR (and risk).
Unit 1 Assessment

quizData.text: How is Effective Gross Income (EGI) calculated?
quizData.explanation: EGI accounts for vacancy losses (and any other income) before operating expenses are deducted to reach NOI.

quizData.text: What is the Internal Rate of Return (IRR)?
quizData.explanation: IRR is the single discount rate that makes the present value of the cash flows equal to the purchase price (NPV = 0).

quizData.text: A mortgage at 5% is used to buy a property with a 6.5% cap rate. This is an example of:
quizData.explanation: The cost of debt (5%) is below the cap rate (6.5%), so borrowing increases the return on equity — positive leverage.

resources.title: Case study — Analyzing a CRE investment

units.title: Real Estate Capital Structures
units.description: How deals are financed with layers of debt and equity — the capital stack, leverage, mezzanine and preferred equity, and the GP/LP waterfall that splits the profits.

sections.content:
## What you'll be able to do

- Rank the layers of the **capital stack** by risk, return and priority of payment.
- Explain **why** investors use debt (three strategic reasons).
- Tell apart **mezzanine debt**, **preferred equity** and **common equity**.
- Read a **GP/LP waterfall** and explain the **promote**.

> Builds on Unit 1 (leverage & levered IRR) and Unit 3 (mortgages).

## The capital stack

Most real estate deals are financed with a **mix** of debt and equity, stacked by priority. From safest/lowest return at the bottom to riskiest/highest return at the top:

- **Senior debt** (the mortgage) — paid first, lowest cost, lowest risk.
- **Mezzanine debt** — subordinate to senior debt, higher rate.
- **Preferred equity** — hybrid; paid before common equity, fixed-ish return.
- **Common equity** (LP + GP) — paid **last**, takes the **first loss**, but captures **all the upside**.

Two rules follow from the order:
1. **Priority of payment** runs bottom-up: senior debt is served before anyone above it.
2. **Risk and return rise as you go up** the stack — the last-paid money demands the highest return.
Knowledge Check — The Stack

quizData.text: In a typical capital stack, which position has the highest risk and the highest expected return?
quizData.explanation: Common equity is the 'first loss' position but captures all the upside, so it commands the highest expected return.

quizData.text: Which layer is paid first from the property's cash flow?
quizData.explanation: Senior lenders have priority of payment (and foreclosure rights) ahead of every other stakeholder.

lessons.title: Why Use Debt? The Leverage Lab

sections.content:
## Three strategic reasons to use debt

1. **Leverage (return on equity).** Unleveraged real estate has limited upside. Borrowing at a cost below the cap rate amplifies the return on your equity — **positive leverage**. (If the cost of debt exceeds the cap rate, leverage turns **negative** and dilutes returns.)
2. **Diversification.** With €10m of equity you can buy two €5m buildings all-cash, or eight €5m buildings at 75% LTV (€40m total) — spreading idiosyncratic risk across more assets.
3. **Inflation hedge.** Fixed-rate debt payments stay flat while rents rise with inflation, so real debt burden shrinks over time.

### Leverage lab

Increase the loan-to-value and watch how leverage amplifies **both** the equity IRR **and** the risk.
Knowledge Check — Leverage

quizData.text: Leverage is 'positive' (it lifts equity returns) when:
options: The cost of debt is below the cap rate
The cost of debt is above the cap rate
quizData.correctAnswer: The cost of debt is below the cap rate
quizData.explanation: When borrowing costs less than the property yields (cap rate), each borrowed euro earns more than it costs — positive leverage.

quizData.text: With €10m equity, moving from all-cash to 75% LTV lets you buy roughly:
quizData.explanation: At 75% LTV your equity covers 25% of each deal, so the same equity controls about four times the asset value — more diversification.

sections.content:
## Beyond senior debt

### Mezzanine debt
- **Position:** subordinate to senior debt, senior to equity.
- **Security:** often secured by a pledge of the **equity interest** in the borrowing entity, not the property itself — so on default the mezz lender can take over the entity (faster than property foreclosure).

### Preferred equity
- **Nature:** a hybrid — part of the ownership stack, but behaves like debt (a fixed preferred return).
- **Priority:** paid before common equity, after all debt.
- **Control:** usually none, unless a 'kick-out' trigger event occurs.

### The GP/LP structure
- **Limited Partner (LP):** provides most of the cash (often 90–95%), limited control.
- **General Partner (GP):** provides expertise, runs the deal, puts in a little capital, and earns a **promote** — a disproportionate share of profits above agreed return hurdles.
Knowledge Check — Structures

quizData.text: Mezzanine debt is typically secured by:
options: A first mortgage on the property
A pledge of the equity interest in the borrowing entity
quizData.correctAnswer: A pledge of the equity interest in the borrowing entity
quizData.explanation: Mezz is usually secured by the ownership interest in the entity, letting the lender take control of the entity on default rather than foreclosing on the property.

quizData.text: The GP's 'promote' is:
options: A share of profits above agreed return hurdles
quizData.correctAnswer: A share of profits above agreed return hurdles
quizData.explanation: The promote (carried interest) rewards the GP with an outsized share of the upside once the LP's return hurdles are met.

sections.content:
## Splitting the profits: the waterfall

Equity profits are distributed through a **waterfall** — a sequence of tiers:

1. **Return of capital** — LPs get their invested capital back.
2. **Preferred return** — LPs receive a hurdle return (e.g. 8%) before the GP shares in profit.
3. **Promote / carried interest** — above the hurdle, the GP takes a larger slice (e.g. 20%) as reward for performance.

Use the simulator to structure the tiers and see how the **promote** shifts the split between LP and GP as returns rise.

## Real-world capital structuring

Apply the unit to a real European deal. Structure the debt and equity, set the waterfall, and see how the capital stack drives investor returns in the Barcelona case.

## One-page summary

- **Capital stack:** senior debt → mezzanine → preferred equity → common equity. Priority of payment runs bottom-up; risk and return rise top-up.
- **Why debt:** leverage (when cost of debt < cap rate), diversification, and an inflation hedge.
- **Mezzanine** is secured by the equity interest; **preferred equity** behaves like debt inside the ownership stack.
- **GP/LP:** LP funds most of the capital; GP runs the deal and earns a **promote** above return hurdles.
- **Waterfall:** return of capital → preferred return → promote.
Unit 2 Assessment

quizData.text: Order these from FIRST to LAST paid: common equity, senior debt, mezzanine debt.
options: Senior debt → mezzanine debt → common equity
Common equity → mezzanine debt → senior debt
Mezzanine debt → senior debt → common equity
quizData.correctAnswer: Senior debt → mezzanine debt → common equity
quizData.explanation: Priority of payment: senior debt first, then mezzanine, with common equity paid last (and bearing first loss).

quizData.text: A deal's senior loan costs 4.5% and the property's cap rate is 6%. The leverage is:
quizData.explanation: Cost of debt (4.5%) is below the cap rate (6%), so borrowing increases the return on equity — positive leverage.

quizData.text: In a waterfall, the LP's preferred return is paid:
options: Before the GP shares in profits
Only if the property is sold at a loss
quizData.correctAnswer: Before the GP shares in profits
quizData.explanation: The LP receives its preferred (hurdle) return before the GP earns any promote on the upside.

resources.title: III.5 Capital Structure class exercise — Excel (empty + solutions)

units.title: Commercial Mortgages
units.description: How commercial loans are structured, how the four repayment patterns behave, and how loans are valued, prepaid, refinanced and underwritten.

sections.content:
## What you'll be able to do

- Distinguish the **promissory note** from the **mortgage** (the collateral instrument).
- Describe the **four repayment patterns** and how each one's payment and balance behave.
- Compute and interpret the two ratios lenders live by — **LTV** and **DSCR**.
- Value a seasoned loan in the secondary market and reason about **prepayment** and **refinancing**.

> Prerequisite: Unit 1 (proforma & NOI) and Unit 0.2 (present-value math).

## The note and the mortgage are two different things

- **Promissory note** — the contract that sets out the financial obligation: principal, interest rate, term and payment schedule. It is the *debt*.
- **Mortgage** (or *deed of trust*) — the legal instrument that pledges the property as **collateral** for the note. It is what gives the lender the right to foreclose if the borrower defaults.

You can sell the note (the cash-flow stream) separately in the secondary market; the mortgage follows it.

## The two ratios lenders underwrite on

- **Loan-to-Value (LTV)** = Loan ÷ Property value. Caps how much they'll lend (e.g. 60–75%). Lower LTV = more equity cushion = safer.
- **Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)** = NOI ÷ Annual debt service. Measures how comfortably the property's income covers the loan payment. Most commercial lenders want **≥ 1.25×**.

A loan must satisfy *both* tests — whichever binds first sets the maximum loan.
Knowledge Check — Loan Basics

quizData.text: What does a DSCR of 1.25× mean?
options: NOI is 25% higher than the debt service
NOI is 25% below the debt service
quizData.correctAnswer: NOI is 25% higher than the debt service
quizData.explanation: DSCR = NOI ÷ debt service. 1.25× means the property generates 25% more income than the loan payment — a safety margin lenders require.

quizData.text: Which instrument pledges the property as collateral?
quizData.explanation: The note is the debt obligation; the mortgage is the security instrument that lets the lender foreclose on the property.

sections.content:
## How the loan is paid back

Every commercial loan follows one of four patterns. They differ in how each payment splits between **interest** and **principal**, and therefore how the **balance** falls.

1. **Interest-Only (IO)** — you pay only interest each period; the full principal is due at maturity (a *balloon*). Lowest payment, no amortization.
2. **Constant Payment Mortgage (CPM)** — the **same total payment** every period (a level annuity). Early payments are mostly interest; over time more goes to principal. This is the classic amortizing loan.
3. **Constant Amortization Mortgage (CAM)** — the **same principal repayment** every period, so the payment *falls* over time as interest on the shrinking balance declines.
4. **Adjustable-Rate Mortgage (ARM)** — the rate resets periodically to an index + margin, so payments move with rates.

### Worked example — CPM payment (in euros)

A **€1,000,000** loan, **5%** annual rate, **25-year** term, paid annually. The constant payment solves the annuity formula (Excel: `=PMT(5%, 25, -1,000,000)`):

- **Annual payment ≈ €70,950**
- Year 1 interest = 5% × €1,000,000 = **€50,000**; principal = 70,950 − 50,000 = **€20,950**
- Year 2 interest is charged on the *lower* balance (€979,050), so a bit **less** interest and **more** principal — and so on. That shifting mix is amortization.

> The **principal repaid in a period = payment − interest owed**. Memorise that one line; the whole schedule falls out of it.

### ARM mechanics

An ARM's rate = **index + margin** (e.g. 12-month Euribor + 2.5%). To protect borrowers, ARMs carry **caps**: an *annual* cap (how much the rate can move per reset) and a *lifetime* cap (the maximum rate ever). When the rate resets up, the payment rises — the core risk of an ARM.
Knowledge Check — Repayment Patterns

quizData.text: Which mortgage has the same total payment every period?
quizData.explanation: CPM is a level annuity — the same payment each period, with the interest/principal split shifting over time.

quizData.text: In any amortizing loan, the principal repaid in a period equals:
quizData.explanation: Whatever part of the payment exceeds the interest due reduces the outstanding balance.

quizData.text: An ARM's interest rate is set as:
quizData.explanation: The fully-indexed rate is a benchmark index (e.g. Euribor) plus a fixed lender margin, subject to caps.

sections.content:
## Loan mechanics lab

Explore how the **interest rate**, **amortization period**, **interest-only period** and **loan amount** shape the payment and the amortization schedule. Watch the interest/principal split shift as the balance falls.
Try it: extend the **interest-only period** and watch the balloon at maturity grow; raise the **rate** and see how much more of each early payment is eaten by interest.

## Valuing a seasoned loan

When a loan is sold in the secondary market, its **price is set by the current market yield**, not its original coupon. Discount the loan's remaining cash flows at today's market rate:

- If **market yield < coupon**, the loan trades at a **premium** (price above the outstanding balance).
- If **market yield > coupon**, it trades at a **discount**.

## Prepayment risk

Borrowers tend to **prepay when rates fall** (to refinance cheaper). If you bought the loan at a premium, early prepayment means you get par back instead of the premium stream — a loss of yield. **Prepayment penalties / yield maintenance** clauses exist to protect the lender against exactly this.

## The refinancing decision (an NPV problem)

Refinance when the present value of the interest **savings** exceeds the **transaction costs** (points + fees) over your expected holding period.

1. Compute the current loan payment (status quo).
2. Compute the new loan payment + closing costs.
3. Compare the PV of the monthly savings against the upfront cost.

> Short remaining hold → fees often outweigh savings (don't refinance). Long hold → savings usually win. It is always a present-value comparison, never just "the new rate is lower."
Knowledge Check — Yields & Refinancing

quizData.text: A loan's 8% coupon is above today's 7% market yield. It will trade at:
quizData.explanation: Its above-market coupon is worth more than par, so buyers pay a premium. Falling market yields push loan prices up.

quizData.text: When are borrowers most likely to prepay?
quizData.explanation: Falling rates make refinancing attractive, so borrowers prepay — the risk that prepayment penalties protect against.

sections.content:
## One-page summary

- **Note = the debt; mortgage = the collateral.**
- **Four patterns:** IO (balloon), CPM (level payment), CAM (level principal, falling payment), ARM (rate = index + margin, with caps).
- **Amortization rule:** principal repaid = payment − interest owed.
- **Underwriting:** a loan must pass both **LTV** and **DSCR (≥ ~1.25×)** — the binding one sets the max loan.
- **Secondary market:** price = PV of remaining cash flows at today's yield; falling yields → premium.
- **Prepayment & refinancing** are present-value decisions; penalties protect lenders against rate-driven prepayment.
Unit 3 Assessment

quizData.text: A property has NOI of €125,000 and the proposed annual debt service is €100,000. What is the DSCR?
quizData.explanation: DSCR = NOI ÷ debt service = 125,000 ÷ 100,000 = 1.25× — right at a typical lender minimum.

quizData.text: In a CAM loan, over time the periodic payment:
quizData.explanation: CAM repays the same principal each period, so interest on the shrinking balance declines and the total payment falls.

quizData.text: You should refinance when:
options: The new rate is lower than the old rate
The PV of interest savings exceeds the transaction costs over your hold
quizData.correctAnswer: The PV of interest savings exceeds the transaction costs over your hold
quizData.explanation: Refinancing is an NPV decision: weigh the present value of savings against points and fees over how long you'll hold.

units.title: Single-Asset Valuation Model
units.description: Put it all together: a full single-asset model with a multi-year proforma, debt, and levered vs unlevered returns.

sections.content:
## From pieces to a complete model

This unit combines everything so far into one institutional-style model for a single property:

1. **Proforma** — project PGI → NOI for the hold period (Unit 1).
2. **Debt** — size a loan by LTV and DSCR, add the mortgage (Unit 3).
3. **Returns** — compute **unlevered** (property) and **levered** (equity) IRR and equity multiple.
4. **Exit** — value the sale with a terminal cap rate and net out selling costs and the loan balance.
5. **Sensitivity** — flex the cap rate, growth, and leverage to see the range of outcomes.

The unlevered IRR tells you about the *asset*; the levered IRR tells you about your *equity* once debt is layered on.
Knowledge Check — The Model

quizData.text: The unlevered IRR measures the return to:
quizData.explanation: Unlevered return strips out financing to measure the asset's own performance.

quizData.text: Adding debt that costs less than the cap rate will:
options: Raise the levered IRR above the unlevered IRR
quizData.correctAnswer: Raise the levered IRR above the unlevered IRR
quizData.explanation: That's positive leverage — cheap debt amplifies equity returns.

sections.content:
## Build and stress-test the model

Drive the full model: set the proforma assumptions, the loan, and the exit, then read the unlevered and levered returns. Push the assumptions to see how fragile or robust the deal is.
> Analyst habit: never trust a single output. Move the exit cap rate up by 50–100 bps and the growth rate down, and check the deal still works. Deals are made or lost in the downside case.

## One-page summary

- A single-asset model = proforma + debt + returns + exit + sensitivity.
- **Unlevered** return = the asset; **levered** return = your equity after financing.
- Positive leverage (debt cost < cap rate) lifts the levered IRR — and the risk.
- Always test the **downside** (higher exit cap, lower growth) before committing.
Unit Assessment — Single-Asset Valuation

quizData.text: Raising the EXIT cap rate, all else equal, makes the value at sale:
quizData.explanation: A higher exit cap rate divides the exit NOI by a bigger number, reducing the sale price.

quizData.text: Equity multiple is:
quizData.explanation: It's the gross multiple of cash returned to the equity versus cash invested.

units.title: Case Study: Valencia & Barcelona
units.description: A real European deal: underwrite the property, structure the financing, and analyse the returns end-to-end.

sections.content:
## The assignment

You are evaluating a real Spanish residential investment. Your job: underwrite the income, structure debt and equity, set an exit, and decide whether the returns justify the risk.

Work through these questions as you use the model:

1. What stabilised **NOI** does the property support, and what **cap rate** is appropriate for this market?
2. How much **debt** can it carry given DSCR and LTV limits — and is the leverage positive?
3. What **hold period** and **exit cap** are realistic, and how sensitive is the IRR to them?
4. How would you split returns between an **LP** and a **GP** in a waterfall?
Pre-case Check

quizData.text: The first thing to pin down when underwriting is:
quizData.explanation: Everything — value, loan sizing, returns — flows from a credible stabilised NOI.

quizData.text: 'Positive leverage' in this deal means:
options: Cost of debt below the cap rate
quizData.correctAnswer: Cost of debt below the cap rate
quizData.explanation: Only then does borrowing raise the equity return.

sections.content:
## Analyse the deal

Use the case model to underwrite the property, size the financing, and read the returns. Test the downside before you conclude.
> Deliverable: a one-paragraph recommendation — buy or pass — citing the levered IRR, the DSCR, and the two assumptions the outcome depends on most.

## What good analysis looks like

A strong recommendation states the **return**, the **risk** (DSCR, sensitivity), and the **key assumptions** it hinges on — not just a single IRR number. Markets like Valencia and Barcelona reward investors who underwrite conservatively and structure financing to survive a softer exit.
Unit Assessment — Case Study

quizData.text: A DSCR of 1.10x on this deal would tell a lender:
options: Thin cushion — income barely covers debt
quizData.correctAnswer: Thin cushion — income barely covers debt
quizData.explanation: 1.10x leaves little margin; most lenders want ≥ 1.25x.

quizData.text: The most useful sensitivity to run on a case like this is:
quizData.explanation: Exit cap and growth drive most of the IRR's variability.

units.title: Affordable Housing Fund
units.description: Step up from one building to a fund: pooled capital, multiple assets, subsidy-supported affordable housing, and a fund-level waterfall.

sections.content:
## Fund-level thinking

A fund pools investor capital to buy **many** assets. That changes the analysis:

- Returns are measured **across the portfolio**, net of **fund fees** and the GP **promote**.
- Capital is **called** over time and **distributed** as assets perform and exit — the classic **J-curve** (early outflows, later inflows).
- Diversification across assets reduces idiosyncratic risk.

### Affordable housing finance
Affordable housing deals are supported by **subsidies and incentives** (tax credits such as LIHTC in the US, and grants/below-market loans and density bonuses in Europe). These lower the cost of capital in exchange for keeping rents below market for a defined period — turning otherwise sub-economic projects into viable, stable, mission-aligned investments.
Knowledge Check — Funds

quizData.text: The fund 'J-curve' describes:
options: Early capital outflows followed by later distributions
quizData.correctAnswer: Early capital outflows followed by later distributions
quizData.explanation: Investors fund the deals first; returns come later as assets stabilise and exit.

quizData.text: Affordable housing subsidies (e.g. tax credits) primarily:
options: Lower the project's cost of capital in exchange for rent restrictions
quizData.correctAnswer: Lower the project's cost of capital in exchange for rent restrictions
quizData.explanation: Subsidies make below-market-rent projects financially viable.

sections.content:
## Model the fund

Set the fund's assets, leverage, fees and waterfall, and read the **LP** and **GP** returns. Watch how the **promote** and fees shift the split, and how subsidy assumptions change viability.
> Note the gap between the **gross** (asset-level) return and the **net** (LP) return — fees and promote live in that gap.

## One-page summary

- A fund pools capital across many assets; returns are measured **net of fees and promote**.
- Capital is called and returned over time — the **J-curve**.
- **Affordable housing** relies on subsidies/incentives that lower the cost of capital in return for rent restrictions.
- Always compare **gross** vs **net (LP)** returns — that gap is the cost of the manager.
Unit Assessment — Affordable Housing Fund

quizData.text: The LP's NET return differs from the gross asset return because of:
options: Fund fees and the GP promote
quizData.correctAnswer: Fund fees and the GP promote
quizData.explanation: Management fees and carried interest are deducted to get the LP's net return.

quizData.text: Diversifying a fund across many assets mainly reduces:
quizData.explanation: Spreading across assets dilutes single-asset shocks; market-wide risk remains.

units.title: Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS)
units.description: How pools of mortgages become tradable bonds — securitization, tranching, credit enhancement, and prepayment risk.

sections.content:
## Turning loans into bonds

A bank holding many commercial mortgages can **securitize** them: pool the loans, place them in a trust, and sell **bonds** backed by the pool's cash flows. These are **Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS)**.

Why do it?
- It moves loans **off the bank's balance sheet**, freeing capital to lend again.
- It lets investors buy real-estate credit at the **risk level they want** via tranching.
- It deepens **liquidity** in the mortgage market.

## Tranching: slicing risk

The pool's cash flows are divided into **tranches** with a strict payment order (the 'waterfall'):

- **Senior tranches (AAA)** are paid first — lowest yield, lowest risk.
- **Mezzanine tranches** absorb losses next — higher yield.
- The **junior / first-loss ('B-piece')** tranche is paid last and absorbs the **first losses** — highest yield.

This is **credit enhancement by subordination**: the junior pieces protect the senior ones. Different investors buy different tranches according to their appetite for risk.
Knowledge Check — CMBS

quizData.text: In a CMBS structure, which tranche absorbs losses first?
quizData.explanation: Subordination means the junior 'B-piece' takes first losses, protecting senior tranches.

quizData.text: A key reason banks securitize loans is to:
options: Move loans off the balance sheet and free up capital
quizData.correctAnswer: Move loans off the balance sheet and free up capital
quizData.explanation: Securitization recycles capital so the bank can originate more loans.

sections.content:
## Prepayment and extension risk

MBS investors face two timing risks:
- **Prepayment risk** — when rates fall, borrowers refinance and repay early, returning principal that must be reinvested at lower rates. Commercial loans often limit this with **prepayment penalties / yield maintenance**.
- **Extension risk** — when rates rise or refinancing is hard, loans stay outstanding longer than expected.

## What 2008 taught

The financial crisis showed how securitization can **mask and spread risk**: weak underwriting, over-reliance on ratings, and complex re-securitizations (CDOs) meant losses were larger and more widely distributed than investors understood. The mechanics are powerful, but they depend entirely on the **quality of the underlying loans** and honest risk assessment.
Knowledge Check — MBS Risk

quizData.text: Prepayment risk hurts an MBS investor most when:
quizData.explanation: Falling rates trigger refinancing/prepayment, forcing reinvestment at lower yields.

quizData.text: A central lesson of 2008 for securitization was:
options: Outcomes depend on the quality of the underlying loans
quizData.correctAnswer: Outcomes depend on the quality of the underlying loans
quizData.explanation: No structure can fix bad underwriting; risk was understated and widely spread.

resources.title: MBS Lecture Notes (Geltner & Miller Ch. 20) — slides

units.title: REITs
units.description: Real Estate Investment Trusts: how they work, the rules that define them, and the metrics used to value them.

sections.content:
## Real estate you can buy like a share

A **REIT** (Real Estate Investment Trust) is a company that owns (and usually operates) income-producing real estate, whose shares trade like any stock. REITs let individual investors hold a diversified, professionally-managed property portfolio with daily liquidity.

### The defining bargain
In exchange for special tax treatment (generally **no corporate tax** on distributed income), a REIT must meet strict tests. The classic US rules — mirrored in spirit by Europe's regimes (the Spanish **SOCIMI**, French SIIC, UK REIT) — require it to:
- hold the large majority of assets in **real estate**,
- earn most income from **rents/real-estate sources**, and
- distribute the large majority of taxable income to shareholders as **dividends** (≈90%).

That high payout is why REITs are prized for **income**.
Knowledge Check — REIT Basics

quizData.text: A REIT generally must distribute to shareholders about:
quizData.explanation: The high mandatory payout is the trade-off for avoiding corporate-level tax.

quizData.text: The main appeal of REITs to individual investors is:
options: Liquid, diversified real-estate exposure with income
quizData.correctAnswer: Liquid, diversified real-estate exposure with income
quizData.explanation: Shares trade daily and pay high dividends from a diversified portfolio.

sections.content:
## Why net income misleads for property

Accounting **depreciation** treats buildings as wearing out, creating a large non-cash expense that depresses reported net income — even as the real estate may be holding or gaining value. So analysts use property-specific measures:

- **FFO (Funds From Operations)** = Net income + real-estate **depreciation & amortization** − gains on property sales. It adds back the non-cash depreciation to better reflect cash earnings.
- **AFFO (Adjusted FFO)** = FFO − recurring **capital expenditures** (and adjustments). A closer proxy for the cash actually available to pay dividends.

REITs are commonly valued on a **price/FFO multiple** (like a P/E for property) and on their **dividend yield**, alongside **NAV** (net asset value of the underlying real estate).
Knowledge Check — REIT Metrics

quizData.text: FFO adds back which non-cash item to net income?
quizData.explanation: Depreciation is a large non-cash charge for property; FFO adds it back.

quizData.text: AFFO differs from FFO mainly by subtracting:
quizData.explanation: AFFO nets out maintenance capex to better reflect distributable cash.

units.title: Real Estate Development Finance
units.description: Financing the creation of new buildings: the development process, the J-curve, construction lending, and feasibility analysis.

sections.content:
## Development is a different game

Development creates value by **building** rather than buying stabilised income. Its cash-flow profile is a **J-curve**: large negative outflows up front (land, design, permits, construction) followed by a sharp positive return at stabilisation or sale.

The stages — **Acquisition → Development (entitlement/design) → Construction → Lease-up/Stabilisation → Exit** — each carry distinct risks. Returns must compensate for **entitlement risk**, **construction risk** (cost and time overruns) and **lease-up risk**.

## Construction (ADC) financing

Development is financed with **Acquisition, Development & Construction (ADC)** loans. Characteristics:
- The loan **draws down** as construction progresses (you borrow as you spend), so interest accrues on a rising balance — often funded by an **interest reserve**.
- It's usually **interest-only** during construction, with a **balloon** at completion, then refinanced into a permanent loan (**'mini-perm' → take-out**).
- Lenders size it on **loan-to-cost (LTC)** as well as loan-to-value, and require the developer's equity in **first**.
Knowledge Check — Development

quizData.text: The development 'J-curve' refers to:
options: Large early outflows, then a later positive return
quizData.correctAnswer: Large early outflows, then a later positive return
quizData.explanation: Land and construction costs come first; returns arrive at stabilisation/exit.

quizData.text: A construction loan typically:
options: Is fully funded on day one
quizData.explanation: Funds are advanced against progress, so interest accrues on a rising balance.

sections.content:
## Will the numbers work?

Developers test feasibility two ways:

- **Front-door** (cost-led): start from total **development cost**, apply the required return, and solve for the **rent you must achieve**. Then ask: is that rent achievable in this market?
- **Back-door** (market-led): start from **achievable market rent**, work back to the value it supports, subtract the developer's required profit, and solve for the **maximum land/cost** you can afford — the **residual land value**.

Both meet in the middle: a deal works only when **value created ≥ total cost + required profit**. The gap between them is the **development margin** (often expressed as profit on cost).

### Real options thinking

Land is partly an **option**: the right, not the obligation, to build. That flexibility — to wait, to phase, to change use — has value, which is why developable land can be worth more than any single immediate use implies.
Knowledge Check — Feasibility

quizData.text: The back-door approach solves for:
options: The maximum affordable land/cost given market rent
quizData.correctAnswer: The maximum affordable land/cost given market rent
quizData.explanation: Back-door works from achievable rent back to residual land value.

quizData.text: A development is feasible when:
options: Value created ≥ total cost + required profit
quizData.correctAnswer: Value created ≥ total cost + required profit
quizData.explanation: Feasibility means the finished value covers all costs plus the developer's profit.

units.title: After-Tax Analysis
units.description: How taxes shape real estate returns: depreciation, after-tax cash flow, capital gains, and tax-deferred exchanges.

sections.content:
## Tax changes the real return

Investors keep the **after-tax** return, so taxes belong in the model. The most powerful lever in real estate is **depreciation**: tax rules let you deduct a portion of the **building's** value (not the land) each year as a non-cash expense. That deduction **shields income from tax**, even when the cash flow is positive and the property is appreciating.

**After-tax cash flow** = before-tax cash flow − taxes, where taxable income = NOI − interest − depreciation. Because depreciation is non-cash, it often turns a taxable profit into a taxable *loss* while the investor still pockets cash — the classic real-estate tax advantage.
Knowledge Check — Depreciation

quizData.text: Depreciation is valuable to a real estate investor because it:
options: Is a cash expense that must be paid
Is a non-cash deduction that shields income from tax
quizData.correctAnswer: Is a non-cash deduction that shields income from tax
quizData.explanation: You deduct it for tax without paying cash, lowering the tax bill.

quizData.text: Depreciation applies to:
quizData.explanation: Land is not depreciable; only the improvements are.

sections.content:
## The tax bill at sale

On sale, gains are taxed in two pieces: **capital gain** (sale price above purchase price) and **depreciation recapture** (the depreciation you previously deducted is partly clawed back). Both reduce the net proceeds — so after-tax IRR is always lower than pre-tax IRR.

## Deferring the tax

Many regimes let investors **defer** the tax by rolling proceeds into another property: the US **1031 like-kind exchange** is the classic example; several European systems offer rollover-relief equivalents. Deferral keeps more capital working and compounding — a major reason long-term investors rarely sell outright. The tax isn't erased, but postponing it has real present-value benefit.
Knowledge Check — Gains & Exchanges

quizData.text: A US 1031 (like-kind) exchange lets an investor:
options: Defer the capital-gains tax by reinvesting in another property
quizData.correctAnswer: Defer the capital-gains tax by reinvesting in another property
quizData.explanation: It postpones (not erases) the gain by rolling into a replacement property.

quizData.text: After-tax IRR compared with pre-tax IRR is generally:
quizData.explanation: Taxes on income and gains reduce the cash the investor keeps, lowering the after-tax return.
